coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-MnO2 based turn-on fluorescent probe for rapid and sensitive detection of hydrazine in water
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Hiremath, Sharanabasava D.
Priyadarshi, Bhaskar
Banerjee, Mainak
Chatterjee, Amrita
coredata.dc:description: Herein, a novel carbon dots-MnO2 assembly based fluorescent probe has been developed for sensitive and rapid detection of hydrazine in aqueous media. The Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET) based sensing platform was devised by adhering fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) on to MnO2 nanosheets in which MnO2 nanosheets act as a quencher and the inherent fluorescence of CDs gets quenched upon interaction with it by electrostatic interaction. This sensing platform selectively responds to hydrazine in the presence of environmentally relevant amines, metal ions, anions and other competing reducing species such as glutathione (GSH). Strong reducing ability of hydrazine effects quick conversion of Mn4+ in the nanosheets to free Mn2+ ions, which is immediately scavenged by glycine. As MnO2 nanosheets break apart the CDs loose free and go to a disperse phase in aqueous solution to emit greenish-blue fluorescence. A sharp rise in fluorescence intensity at 445 nm was observed upon gradual addition of hydrazine with a linearity range from 0.15 to 1.5 μM and a detection limit of 4.8 ppb for hydrazine was estimated which is below the US-EPA set threshold limit of 10 ppb. This cost-effective sensing tool with complete avoidance of toxic organic solvents for synthesis and fluorimetric studies coupled with high selectivity and sensitivity, rapid detection, low detection limit is a much viable alternative to existing detection tools for on-site detection and quantitation of hydrazine.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
MnO2nanosheets
Hydrazine sensing
Organic-solvent-free detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CDs embedded MnO2 nanosheets
Synthesis and characterization of the CDs, MnO2 nanosheets and their assembly
Determination of quantum yield of the CDs
Quenching of CDs with MnO2 nanosheets
Design of sensing platform and fluorimetric detection of hydrazine
Mechanistic illustration of the sensing process
Detection of hydrazine in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: UGC-DAE Consortium for Scientific Research, University Grants Commission

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) AFM image and graph of the average size of CDs; b) FT-IR of the CDs; c) TEM image of CDs.

a) Blue line corresponds to UV–vis spectrum of CDs, red line corresponds to excitation spectra and green line corresponds to emission spectra of CDs; b) Fluorescence response of CDs recorded at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 to 540 with gradual increment of 20 nm.
a) UV–vis spectrum of the MnO2 nanosheets in comparison with KMnO4; b) Fluorescence response of CDs and CDs-MnO2 assembly; TEM images of c) MnO2 nanosheets, d) CDs-MnO2 assembly.

a) Fluorimetric response of CDs-MnO2 assembly upon addition of hydrazine (0–45 μM) in water [λex 365 nm; λem 445 nm]. Inset: A plot of relative fluorescence enhancement vs concentration of hydrazine (0–45 μM).
Maximum fluorescence response of CDs-MnO2 assembly in the presence of various interfering analytes (40 μM) in water (λex 365 nm; λem 445 nm).
The fluorimetric response of varies reducing analytes (40 μM) towards CDs-MnO2 assembly under optimised conditions (λex 365 nm; λem 445 nm).
A plot of relative fluorescence intensities of CDs-MnO2 assembly against concentration of hydrazine [concentration range: 0.15–1.5 μM].
Maximum fluorescence response of CDs-MnO2 assembly towards real samples spiked with measured amount of hydrazine [λex 365 nm; λem 445 nm].
Fluorescence response of the sensing platform over number of cycles towards reusability study.
Schematic representation of the sensing process.
Determination of hydrazine in real samples.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-MnO2 based turn-on fluorescent probe for rapid and sensitive detection of hydrazine in water

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, BITS, Pilani- K. K. Birla Goa Campus, NH 17B Bypass Road, Zuarinagar, Goa, 403726, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: BITS, Pilani- K. K. Birla Goa Campus

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, BITS, Pilani- K. K. Birla Goa Campus, NH 17B Bypass Road, Zuarinagar, Goa 403726, INDIA.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a novel carbon dots-MnO2 assembly based fluorescent probe has been developed for sensitive and rapid detection of hydrazine in aqueous media. The Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET) based sensing platform was devised by adhering fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) on to MnO2 nanosheets in which MnO2 nanosheets act as a quencher and the inherent fluorescence of CDs gets quenched upon interaction with it by electrostatic interaction. This sensing platform selectively responds to hydrazine in the presence of environmentally relevant amines, metal ions, anions and other competing reducing species such as glutathione (GSH). Strong reducing ability of hydrazine effects quick conversion of Mn4+ in the nanosheets to free Mn2+ ions, which is immediately scavenged by glycine. As MnO2 nanosheets break apart the CDs loose free and go to a disperse phase in aqueous solution to emit greenish-blue fluorescence. A sharp rise in fluorescence intensity at 445 nm was observed upon gradual addition of hydrazine with a linearity range from 0.15 to 1.5 μM and a detection limit of 4.8 ppb for hydrazine was estimated which is below the US-EPA set threshold limit of 10 ppb. This cost-effective sensing tool with complete avoidance of toxic organic solvents for synthesis and fluorimetric studies coupled with high selectivity and sensitivity, rapid detection, low detection limit is a much viable alternative to existing detection tools for on-site detection and quantitation of hydrazine.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrazine is an essential and widely used chemical in industries for the preparation of pesticides, textile dyes, emulsifiers, polymers, etc. [1,2]. It has also been extensively used as a propellant in rocket launching systems because of its high heat of combustion [3–5]. In spite of its pervasive industrial applications, hydrazine is also well documented for its acute toxicity [6–8], and hydrazine can readily get absorbed by humans causing serious damage to kidney, liver and central nervous systems [9,10]. On account of its high toxicity and high carcinogenicity [11,12], hydrazine is classified as a group B2 chemical by US EPA and its threshold limit has been set as 10 ppb [13,14]. Its high toxicity and the impact on human health and the environment necessitate the development of selective and sensitive methods for the detection of hydrazine maintaining biocompatibility. A large variety of techniques are available for the detection of hydrazine including conventional techniques such as spectrophotometry [15], electrochemical method [16], chromatographic techniques [17,18] and several other modern techniques like nanoparticle-based sensors [19], paper-based sensors [20], among others [21–25]. However, these detection techniques experience shortcoming like less-sensitive, being expensive, time-consuming, the requirement of sophisticated instrumentation and skilled personnel, which causes intricacy for real-time and on-site monitoring. In recent years, fluorimetry has been conceived as one of the better methods for the environmental monitoring of toxic analytes [26–31] and use of a variety of fluorophores have been well-documented for hydrazine by different research groups due to its high sensitivity and selectivity [32–38].

Recently, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) as promising carbon-based nanomaterial have drawn enormous research interests owing to their eco-friendly synthesis from biomass, high water solubility, steady fluorescence, quantum yield, low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility and its passive nature towards a wide variety of chemicals [39–41]. As compared to conventional fluorophores, other carbon-based nanomaterials such as graphene and carbon nitride, and other toxic transition metal-based quantum dots, these exceptional properties bestowed CDs with impending applications in various fields like catalysis [42,43], sensing [44,45], bioimaging [46,47], and so on [48].

Relevant to this work, manganese dioxide (MnO2) nanosheets with their 2D structure, excellent environmental compatibility, and high surface area have received broad research interests [49]. In recent past, it has been utilized as sensors [50–52], nano-quenchers [53,54], adsorbents [55,56], catalysts [57], etc. [58]. Interestingly, a combination of CDs and MnO2 nanosheets has been used as a FRET-based sensing platform [59–68] for various environmentally and biologically relevant reducing species in aqueous media based on the fact that the MnO2 nanosheets would be decomposed by reacting with the reducing species to release fluorescent CDs in solution. Based on this strategy, a series of analytes including GSH [59–61], alkaline phosphate (ALP) [62], organophosphorus (OP) [63], uric acid [64,65], ascorbic acid [66,67], biothiols [68] were selectively detected using carbon dots-MnO2 assembly based sensing platform mostly by identifying a suitable sensing conditions.

Encouraged by the above mentioned literature, we, in continuation of our research interests in the development of fluorescent sensors for environmentally toxic analytes [29–32], herein, report the development of an efficient fluorescence sensing platform for the selective and sensitive detection of hydrazine by exploiting FRET-based CDs-MnO2 assembly. It was envisaged that strong reducing ability of hydrazine would selectively convert water dispersible MnO2 nanosheets to water-soluble Mn2+ ions even in the presence of other competitors at the ideal sensing condition resulting in a turn-on fluorescence response from CDs that are again released into the water.

Solanum tuberosum (potato) was procured from the market, Vasco, Goa, India. Hydrazine monohydrate (N2H4, 64–65 %) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, India. Morpholinoethane sulphonic acid monohydrate (MES buffer, 99%) and potassium permanganate (KMnO4) were purchased from LOBA Chemie, Pvt. Ltd., India. All other chemicals and solvents used were obtained from different commercial suppliers, were of analytical grade and were used directly without further purification. All aqueous solutions were prepared with deionized water produced using Millipore water (18 MΩ).

Fluorescence spectra were taken on a JASCO FP-6300 spectrofluorometer; the slit width was 2.5 nm for both excitation and emission. For fluorescence measurements the excitation wavelength was set to 365 nm and emission spectra were recorded in the range of 366–650 nm. Transmission electron micrograph (TEM) images were recorded on a Philips CM-200 (Operating voltages: 20-200kv, Resolution: 2.4 Ao). IR spectra were recorded on an IR Affinity-1 FTIR Spectrophotometer, Shimadzu. UV–vis spectra were taken on a JASCO V-550, the bandwidth and data pitch were set as 1 nm. Surface morphologies were recorded on atomic force microscope, NT-MDT make.

The CDs were prepared through the hydrothermal treatment of potato pulp (Solanum tuberosum) as carbon source [46]. Briefly, after peeling off 100 g of Solanum tuberosum was ground in a domestic mixer grinder and 50 mL of water was added to the acquired pulp and then it was ground further. Next, the pulpy solution was filtered and the filtrate (50 mL) was taken in a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, and heated for 12 h at 200 °C to obtain a dark brown solution. The resulting solution was filtered through 0.45 nm filter paper and the filtrate was washed with dichloromethane. The aqueous layer was centrifuged and subsequent supernatant was dialyzed in benzoylated cellulose tubing (MWCO 2000) for 12 h against deionized water. Three of the 50 mL batches were separately freeze-dried to obtain 18.1 mg of CDs on an average, which was re-suspended in same volume of water and stored at 4 °C and used as a stock solution for analytical studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs embedded MnO2 nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs embedded MnO2 nanosheets were prepared by mixing a stock solution of CDs with aqueous KMnO4 in MES buffer [65]. Briefly, stock solution of CDs (100 μL), MES buffer (0.5 M, pH 6, 100 μL), KMnO4 (0.1 M, 50 μL) were mixed in 750 μL of ultrapure water for preparation of every 1 mL of sensing solution. The solution was sonicated at room temperature for 30 min to obtain a brown solution. This was centrifuged at 7000 rpm and the resulting precipitate was collected and washed three times with deionized water to wash off excess KMnO4 and re-suspended in 1 mL of ultrapure water by sonication for 5 min.

A 1 mM standard solution of hydrazine monohydrate was prepared in water and used for fluorimetric studies. To 0.5 mL of sensing solution 40 μL of glycine (1 mM) was added followed by addition of required volume of hydrazine (1 mM) and it was further diluted by deionized water (Milli-Q, 18 MΩ) to 1 mL of total volume and fluorescence intensities were recorded immediately. In order to investigate the selectivity of the sensing assembly towards hydrazine, stock solutions of different reducing agents, amino acids, amines (1 mM), chlorides and nitrates of cations (1 mM) and sodium salts of anions (1 mM) were prepared. For real sample analysis the solutions were prepared by spiking known concentration of hydrazine in water samples collected from various water bodies (pond, Zuari river, tap water, paddy field water). All solutions were subjected to filtration through 0.22 μm syringe filter to avoid any interference by any particulate matter in fluorescence measurement. All experiments were carried out at room temperature (25 °C), repeated thrice and the average data were reported.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the CDs, MnO2 nanosheets and their assembly
ce:section.ce:para: The ultrathin MnO2 nanosheets were prepared by reduction of KMnO4 with MES buffer [65]. The formation of MnO2 nanosheets was initially confirmed by UV − vis spectroscopy followed by TEM and AFM analysis (Fig. 3 , Fig. S1 of SI). AFM graph revealed that the average thickness of MnO2 nanosheets are 4−6 nm (Fig. S1 of SI). A broad UV − vis absorption band in the range from 300 to 500 nm indicated the presence of MnO2 nanosheets and the absence of KMnO4 (Fig. 3a). The CDs-MnO2 assembly was prepared just by mixing a stock solution of CDs with aqueous KMnO4 in MES buffer and sonicating the solution at room temperature for 30 min [65]. As a preliminary examination, fluorescence responses of CDs and CDs-MnO2 assembly were separately recorded, which showed quenching of fluorescence intensity of CDs to a minimum level by adhering onto MnO2 nanosheets (Fig. 3b). The morphology of MnO2 nanosheets and CDs-MnO2 assembly were examined by TEM analysis (Fig. 3c,d). The images showed that the presence of large two-dimensional MnO2 nanosheets (Fig. 3c) and clear adherence of CDs onto MnO2 nanosheets (Fig. 3d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of quantum yield of the CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The quantum yield of CDs was determined against standard solution of quinine sulfate (0.1 M in 0.1 M of dil. H2SO4 as a solvent; n = 1.33) with quantum yield of 54%. The following equation was used for calculating fluorescence quantum yield of CDs in water.
φ x
=
φ
s t d
I x
A x
A
s t d
I x
n x 2
n
s t d
2
where φ, I and n represent the quantum yield, slope of the measured integrated emission intensity and refractive index, and A is the optical density. The subscript ‘std’ denotes to standard quinine sulfate and ‘x’ denotes to CDs. The average quantum yields of various batches of as-synthesized CDs were determined as 16.87% with less than 0.5% of deviation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching of CDs with MnO2 nanosheets
ce:section.ce:para: As presented in Fig. S2 of SI, the MnO2 nanosheets show a broad UV–vis spectrum from 200 nm to 600 nm, which is nicely overlapping with fluorescence spectrum of CDs. Thus, in this CDs-MnO2 assembly, MnO2 nanosheets act as the quencher of the fluorescence of CDs [59–68]. Upon the addition of CDs into KMnO4 solution in the presence of MES buffer, MnO2 nanosheets are formed quickly and CDs get adsorbed onto the surface of MnO2 nanosheets by electrostatic interactions to form a CDs-MnO2 assembly. The shorter distance between MnO2 and CDs in the closely packed CDs-MnO2 assembly ensure electron donation and because of FRET the fluorescence of carbon dots is quenched significantly. The zeta potential values of CDs, MnO2 nanosheets and the CDs-MnO2 assembly were measured to understand the possibilities of electrostatic interactions in the assembly. The CDs, prepared from potato pulp, exhibited a relatively high negatively charged surface (-10.96 mV) [70] but MnO2 nanosheets showed a much lower negative zeta potential values (-1.01 mV) [65]; when the CDs got adhered onto MnO2 nanosheets, the value became -1.43 mV (Fig. S3, see SI). The changes in the values of zeta potential firmly indicate that the CDs get adsorbed on the surface of MnO2 nanosheets by electrostatic interaction. An experiment was conducted to optimize the concentration of KMnO4 required against a fixed volume of CDs (100 μL) to obtain highest fluorescence quenching. As shown in fig. S4 of SI, quenching efficiency increases gradually with the increase in the concentrations of KMnO4. When the concentration of KMnO4 was over 4 mM the quenching efficiency was maximum (up to 84%), suggesting that enough MnO2 nanosheets are present in the aqueous solution for the available CDs to impart fluorescence quenching by electrostatic interactions. Thus, 4 mM KMnO4 solution was chosen as optimal concentration for the generation of MnO2 nanosheets for further studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design of sensing platform and fluorimetric detection of hydrazine
ce:section.ce:para: At the outset, the CDs-MnO2 based sensing platform remains at turn-off state due to FRET. It was envisaged that the strong reducing ability of hydrazine would quickly convert MnO2 to water-soluble Mn2+. As MnO2 nanosheets break apart CDs would be released and stay in a disperse phase in the aqueous solution to emit greenish-blue fluorescence. To demonstrate the preconceived phenomenon, the responses of this sensing system at different concentrations of hydrazine were measured. The maximum fluorescence was observed within 2 min of addition of hydrazine. Initially, the fluorescence response was less (2.5 folds) and increment was not linear. Based on this observation, we considered a possibility of formation of water insoluble Mn(OH)2 at the close proximity of CDs which may eventually deposit on CDs to inhibit fluorescence response. Accordingly, addition of few additives in the solution was envisaged which would produce water soluble Mn2+ complex and allow a smooth release of CDs. Among many, glycine was chosen as the first additive to try based on the fact that Mn(II)-bis-glycinate is water soluble [71,72] and would help in getting uninterrupted fluorescence output from CDs (Fig. S5, SI). As shown in Fig. 4 , the fluorescence signal of CDs was slowly restored as the concentration of hydrazine increased in the presence of 40 μM of glycine, which reached saturation when the concentration of hydrazine increased to 30 μM. The fluorescence output became better from 2.5 folds to 5 folds and a good linearity was observed between the fluorescence intensity of CDs and the concentration of hydrazine in the range of 0–30 μM with regression coefficient, R2 = 0.99211 (inset, Fig. 4). The pH of the resulting solution was recorded as 6.8. Couple of more additives (viz. NaOAc and EDTA) were tried for the same purpose and the maximum fluorescence output upon treatment of 40 μM of hydrazine was measured (Fig. S6, SI). As the highest output was obtained with glycine, it was considered as the best additive for the sensing system. Next, the stability of the sensing platform was examined at different pH. It was observed that the fluorescence output of CDs-MnO2 assembly in the absence of hydrazine remains negligible in the whole range of pH (pH 3–10), which confirms both stability and aqueous dispersibility of CDs-MnO2 assembly in a wide range of pH (Fig. S7, SI). The CDs-MnO2 sensing platform showed considerably high fluorescence response in a fairly broad range of pH (pH 6–8) upon introduction of hydrazine with the highest response at near neutral pH (pH 6.8). This gives a window for the CDs-MnO2 assembly to function as a sensing platform for hydrazine to work in mild acidic or basic conditions. Presumably, the lower response in strongly acidic medium is due to the fact that hydrazine remains protonated. On the other hand, a strong basic medium ensures the formation of water insoluble Mn(OH)2 which may inhibit the sensing process. Interestingly, the brown colour of MnO2 nanosheets can be helpful for visual detection of hydrazine as well. As seen in Fig. S8A of SI, the brown colour of the sensing solution fades away with incremental addition of hydrazine and finally becomes colourless on saturation (at 30 μM) making the sensing system capable of visual detection of hydrazine at higher concentrations. Similarly, the gradual fluorescence enhancement can also be seen under long-wave UV lamp (365 nm, Fig. S8B, SI). Thus, CDs-MnO2 assembly could act as a fluorescent as well as colorimetric sensing platform for hydrazine.

The selectivity of CDs-MnO2 ensemble based fluorescence assay toward hydrazine was investigated by competing it with various anions, cations which are commonly abundant in water and also with various amines and reducing agents. Keeping the sensing condition same, 40 μM aqueous solutions of various L-amino acids including alanine (Ala), phenyl alanine (Phe), argenine (Arg), leucine (Leu), poline (Pro), histidine (His), several aliphatic and aromatic amines such as amines diphenylamine (DPA), ethylenediamine (en), trimethylamine (TEA), ethanol amine (EA), other common chemicals like phenylhydrazine, NH4OH, urea, thiourea, hydroxylamine, oxidants like H2O2, sodium hypochlorite, metal ions like Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Ag+ etc., and finally anions like CO3 2−, F-, Cl-, Br-, I-, SO4 2−, NO2-, N3-, NO3- etc. were added to a solution of CDs-MnO2 in water and the fluorescence output of the system was measured (Fig. 5 ). The fluorescence responses of all the solutions other than hydrazine were found to be very nominal. In many cases a slight quenching of fluorescence intensities were observed upon addition of electrolytes. This study reveals that the CDs-MnO2 sensing assay is not only highly efficient for the detection of hydrazine but also exhibits commendable selectivity towards it. The assembly could also perceive hydrazine ions in the presence of all other species (40 μM) with similar efficiency. It infers that none of the different classes of possible interfering species actually inhibit or expedite the redox reaction between hydrazine and MnO2.

A more intrigue selectivity study was done by challenging hydrazine with various naturally occurring easily oxidisable species which may compete with hydrazine in the decomposition of CDs-MnO2 nanosheets to interfere with the fluorescence signal output. As mentioned earlier, CDs-MnO2 based sensing systems already exist reducing for species like GSH [59–61], organophosphorus (OP) [63] ascorbic acid [66,67] cysteine (Cys) [68]. Although co-existence of hydrazine and most of these contending species is highly unlikely in natural water bodies or pertinent industrial wastewater, we felt, it is always worthy to study the selectivity of this sensing tool towards hydrazine in the presence of other reducing agents under the prevailing sensing condition. It is noteworthy to mention that the selectivity of a specific analyte for a CDs-MnO2 based sensing tool often rely on the choice of buffer and sensing condition [see Table S1 of SI]. As seen in Fig. 6 , the naturally occurring reducing agents including lactic acid, glucose, sodium salt of ascorbic acid, biothiol (e.g. cysteine) do not show any appreciable response upon their addition to the sensing solution containing CDs-MnO2 nanosheets. Even the response of glutathione under the prevailing sensing condition is little as compared to hydrazine. The studies indicate that the CDs-MnO2 based sensing tool under the optimum sensing condition in the presence of glycine as additive is selective to hydrazine. In another study, equimolar GSH was challenged with hydrazine and corresponding fluorescence was measured (Fig. S9, SI). Thus, fluorescence output of standard CDs-MnO2 nanosheets solutions containing a) 15 μM GSH, b) 15 μM hydrazine, c) 15 μM GSH and 15 μM hydrazine d) 30 μM GSH e) 30 μM hydrazine, f) 30 μM GSH and 30 μM hydrazine were plotted (Fig. S9, SI). It was observed that the presence of equimolar GSH hardly vary the fluorescence output indicating hydrazine easily prevails over GSH in reducing the MnO2 nanosheets to Mn2+ ions. However, as reported earlier [59], the response of GSH in a suitable buffer solution is more as compared to the sensing condition of the present work (Fig. S10, SI). Overall, selectivity studies revealed that the CDs-MnO2 based sensing tool is highly selective to hydrazine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanistic illustration of the sensing process
ce:section.ce:para: The plausible sensing process is schematically shown in scheme 1 . The MnO2 nanosheets readily adhere negatively charged CDs through electrostatic interaction. Once the closely packed CDs-MnO2 assembly is formed, the fluorescence of CDs could be effectively quenched by FRET [62–65,67]. Further, on introduction of hydrazine to the CDs-MnO2 assembly, hydrazine could spontaneously effect the decomposition of the MnO2 nanosheets by reducing MnO2 into Mn2+, due to large difference in redox potential between MnO2/Mn2+ (+1.23 V) [65] and hydrazine (−1.49 V) [73], which is subsequently scavenged by glycine by forming water soluble complex. This would release CDs into water in the disperse phase to retrieve the fluorescence property. Therefore, the intensity of the fluorescence at 445 nm gradually increases with the increasing concentration of hydrazine. The decomposition of MnO2 nanosheets from CDs-MnO2 assembly was also established by UV − vis absorption spectra of the sensing system in occurrence of different concentrations of hydrazine. It was perceived that with the increase in hydrazine concentration in the solution, the absorption of MnO2 at 400 nm decreases slowly, signifying the decomposition of MnO2 nanosheets into water-soluble Mn2+ complex (Fig. S11, SI).

In a separate analysis, the available fluorophores for hydrazine detection with respect to sensitivity, linear range and detection limit were compared in Table S2, SI. It could be observed that the present sensing platform is better in many aspects than that of the previously reported sensors. Other than low LOD, immediate response and organic-solvent-free detection are notable attributes of this sensing system over others.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of hydrazine in real samples

ce:section.ce:para: With the knowledge that after one round of sensing cycle CDs retrieve back the fluorescence which are again available for FRET-quenching upon interaction with MnO2 nanosheets, a recyclability study of this sensing platform was envisaged. After each cycle a concentrated solution of MnO2 nanosheets was added and similar fluorescence quenching was measured. This was followed by the addition of 40 μM of hydrazine solution. Again, the fluorescence of the resulting solution was turned-on (Fig. 9 ). A drop of maximum fluorescence output upon addition of hydrazine was seen from fourth cycle onwards (Fig. 9). Therefore, the CDs-MnO2 based sensing solution can be easily reused up to the third cycle of hydrazine detection. Even though the preparation of this sensing platform is easy and cost-effective, we felt, the reusability can be an important attribute to this sensing system.

In summary, carbon dots on MnO2 nanosheets was demonstrated as a Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET)-based sensing platform for sensitive and selective detection of hydrazine in aqueous media. CDs were prepared by one-step hydrothermal process from potato pulp (Solanum tuberosum) and MnO2 nanosheets by reduction of KMnO4 by MES buffer. The as-prepared CDs, MnO2 nanosheets and CDs-MnO2 assembly were characterized using TEM, UV–vis and FT-IR spectroscopy. The negatively charged CDs adhere on the surface of MnO2 nanosheets by electrostatic interaction and the resulting assembly remains in fluorescence-quenched state by FRET. On addition of hydrazine to the CDs-MnO2 assembly, fluorescence of CDs get restored as hydrazine reduces MnO2 to water soluble Mn2+ complex by interacting with glycine. The newly developed sensing platform is highly selective and sensitive to hydrazine and shows the detection limit as low as 4.8 ppb which is below the US-EPA set threshold limit of 10 ppb. Moreover, the practical application of the sensing platform was validated through real samples with satisfactory results. Thus, a simple and cost-effective sensing platform was developed for the fluorimetric detection of hydrazine with complete avoidance of toxic organic solvents for synthesis and fluoremetric studies. The CDs-MnO2 assembly with high selectivity and sensitivity, fast response and low detection limit is a potential alternative to existing detection tools for on-site detection and quantitation of hydrazine.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: A.C. thanks UGC-DAE (CSR) (project no. CSR-KN/CRS-91/2016-17/1132) and DST-SERB (India) (project no. EMR/2016/001416) for financial support. S.D.H. is indebted to UGC-DAE for research fellowship. The authors also acknowledge SAIF-IITB, Mumbai, India for TEM images and DST-FIST (No. SR/FST/CSI-232-2011) for AFM facilities in department of chemistry and central instrumentation facility, BITS Pilani KK Birla Goa Campus for zeta potential measurements.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2019.112258.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthetic methodology for alkyl substituted hydrazines
Chem. Soc. Rev.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: U. Ragnarsson, Synthetic methodology for alkyl substituted hydrazines, Chem. Soc. Rev. 30 (2001) 205–213.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrazine and Its Derivatives in Kirk-Othmer Encyclopedia of Chemical Technology

Aviat. Space Environ. Med.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Direct hydrazine fuel cells: a review
Appl. Catal. B

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Serov, C. Kwak, Direct hydrazine fuel cells: A review, Appl. Catal. B 98 (2010) 1–9.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrazine detection using a tyrosinase-based inhibition biosensor
